sentences.text: What helped me?
During the war, what helped me?
I think I was too strong.
My-- my late wife, she told me, remember one thing.
For me is better when you will be alive [? lieutenant. ?] And don't like to have a died general.
You remember.
Don't be a big hero in the front.
You look about yourself.
Don't be a hero, told me, my-- my late wife.
So I was-- I don't say I-- look.
No.
I go normal than the other people.
I don't make some heroism or this in the front, in the-- the-- And you were saying you thought your watch-- watch-- you were very lucky you had your watch.
Yes.
Why I had money.
So when the other people have nothing to eat, not money, I have still money to buy something on the black market.
Everything was on the black market.
Normally, in a shop you come in Russia in this time, nothing to buy.
Not only-- only this time, after the war.
In Russia, was-- the life was difficult.
I was in Russia in 1956 just am a tourist-- was in Moscow and this time in Leningrad.
Again, still everything on the black market.
When you go on the street or in the hotels, when we leave have you something to sell or something.
Always in a normal shop, you can buy nothing.
No.
No.
I was happy to leave Zilina for Prague.
Well, ?] I have a lot friends from the army in Prague still.
There are no-- [? what I ?] was last year, there are a few still there.
And secondly, I have the nephew comes to Prague.
He stay with me, with us, I think five years.
After, he married and [? live ?] [? with ?] [INAUDIBLE].
They have two children and grandchildren.
The nephew [INAUDIBLE] 70-years -old.
Is from my second oldest brother, the son.
It is only one nephew in Czechoslovakia.
Here in Britain, I have three niece.
And in Israel, one nephew from [? our ?] late brother in Israel.
No.
What ?] I must say, the non-Jewish and the Jewish [? they ?] always are Tausinger.
They don't change their name.
She worked at home, not for some company.
They save bras and how you call when you take the women?
Girdles.
So it was origin her profession.
No, not private.
Under the communist, you can do some private for some firm.
No.
She do it at home.
She make it and I took it to [? them. ?] [?
They ?] pay for that.
The shirt?
Yes.
She made for the people.
And ?] [? it ?] [? was ?] Tajikistan or in Voroshilovgrad.
It was so interesting this-- when we was in Voroshilovgrad a short time, we must go once a in a week to the [INAUDIBLE].
Personally they give a stamp in this paper that we are there.
So my-- my wife must go, and this officer, the lady, she knows the-- so-- and she tell to do something for her.
Said, all right.
I can do it.
Now, ?] when I come here, I loose a day every week.
How-- I can do nothing.
Said, ?] don't worry.
I will come to you.
So this officer from the interior police, she come to us, bring the this.
But she need something to do for her.
It is this.
Well, some interesting-- we had been free.
No, not free.
In the army, it was like always in a war, troubles and more troubles, less troubles.
Sometime I was between the [INAUDIBLE], the Czech, and the Russian commanders.
I was in between.
So I go.
And once, I remember-- I don't remember who-- it was the first time.
I spoke with the officer [? in ?] [? the ?] Russian commander.
They started talking, and he told me, I am a Jewish.
It was the first what I have seen a Russian commander they Jewish.
He told me he's a Jewish.
I can see the uniform.
He told me when he knows I am a Jewish.
I don't know how.
So it was the first high ranking officer I-- I have seen a Jewish.
Huh?
No.
No.
Mostly the people from the army what I know and also mixed up-- Jewish, mixed, non-Jewish.
What I said, in Prague the life was, for me, depends.
When they need me, I was on the top.
When they don't need, they pushed me down.
In the last year, it was so bad, they push me out of Prague.
They want somebody my flat.
So I work out of Prague.
So I stay up every morning 5:00 to use the train outside Prague.
And there where I work, they always tell me come and change the flat.
I give you my flat here and I build you a flat in Prague.
So I don't agree with this.
I always fight against.
But when I go out from Prague, it's finished.
Is finished a study, finish everything.
So long I stay in Prague is more or less all right.
It's better for me to use the train without Prague than leave Prague.
But it was a nice flat.
And was it difficult to-- for your sister to get you a visa to England?
In 1968, I was on the British consul in Prague.
And I told the truth.
I have children there.
I will go there.
So they give me a so-called tourist visa for me and my wife.
So I buy the air ticket.
No return.
So I come to Britain.
And in Heathrow, where they checked, he look in my passport.
And [? then, ?] you have a-- you can stay 28 days.
Stay here.
No.
I tell you.
When you change your mind, you don't wait for the last day.
At least a week or 10 days I go to the Home Office, you like to stay here.
So after three weeks, I go to the Home Office and say I will stay here.
So they give me the permission and give me the [INAUDIBLE] for a year I can stay.
After a year-- yes, again, I was under control.
I don't give move in this first year.
When I move, I must tell them.
So I don't move.
I was still in the same place, no problem.
And after a year, I ask, and they give me-- how you call it?
I can stay in Britian, this card.
So after [? that ?] I was free.
I can move where I want.
I got permission to stay in Britain.
After five years, I ask for British citizenship.
First, comes my wife.
She died in between-- in '73.
There come a lady from the Home Office to the flat where I live.
It was in [INAUDIBLE].
He ?] looked around [? her ?] and ask have you a bank account and so on.
And so-- about nothing.
Talking about nothing.
No.
Mostly looking.
I ask her, can I offer you a cup of tea or coffee?
No, thank you.
She don't touch nothing.
So she was there a half hour, hour.
She asks so around nothing.
So when I was asked for my British citizen, I was invited to the Home Office.
There come two men.
So, again, my English was bad.
No. ?] They have Information where I work.
They told me the Director of the Home Office was asking about me.
So they ask around.
Again, from Czechoslovakia.
And the-- and the silly question was, where have you been when started the German Czechoslovak War?
I'm sorry, Gentlemen.
I don't remember about the war.
I must tell you the German took with help from [? this ?] Prime Minister, I think Chamberlain-- took over Czechoslovakia without a war.
It wasn't a war.
The border was open with help from Britain to take over Czechoslovakia.
They look one with the other one.
They don't say a word.
So it was finished.
And in a half a year, I got this certificate for the British citizen.
I must go to the out to swear for the King-- Queen-- that we are true British citizen.
And again, I come back to the work.
I say, I am very proud I am a British citizen.
So [? the ?] one man [INAUDIBLE], a Englishman, he was a nice man, said Albert, I tell you true.
When you open your mouth and started to talk, you will be always a bloody British foreigner.
So it was my British experience.
When you open your mouth and say two words, you are always a bloody British foreigner.
It is true, yes?
When I go speak with somebody and this-- started to work, the next question, where are you from originally?
What did you bring with you from Czechoslovakia?
What I bring?
Nothing.
What I have on me.
Nothing.
Some pictures [? whatever. ?] Nothing.
I was-- I leave the flat in the night to airport.
And there, I wait always with [INAUDIBLE] And the airport there was Russian soldiers.
By these soldiers, you never know what they will-- will do.
No.
Luckily not-- not.
So we-- what I said, we used the plane to Heathrow.
And this gentlemen, this British, he was very nice.
And what he say, don't wait the last day.
You must go at least a week, 10 days before.
Ask for permission to stay in Britain.
So I got a certificate from one year.
And after-- I told you-- after a year, I go to-- how you say it?
I had a [? full-- ?] not I'm a British citizen.
I free.
I can move from one place another without telling to the police.
Yes.
It was in 1963.
I got the permission to fly to London and Israel.
Only one thing-- I must go alone.
My wife and the children must stay in Prague.
So I fly to London.
My-- my brother-in-law and my sister, they took me to Israel, to Tel Aviv.
After 30 years, I see on the airport my brother.
After 30 years.
So in the first moment, like two strangers.
It's a 30 years age difference.
After, he ask about the mother.
How was the mother when she died?
And from this moment when he ask about the mother, it was again two brothers.
Interesting.
You don't see 30 years somebody.
You don't have a chance to this.
So after this moment when he ask about the mother, we were two brothers again.
Nobody know.
No.
After '68 when I was British citizen, I was many times in Israel.
In the marriage, we was together-- I don't-- three or four times in [?
Eilat. ?] We fly London to Tel Aviv.
I stay with my brother a few days.
And I-- with a coach, we go to [?
Eilat. ?] And from [?
Eilat, ?] direct to London.
My wife, she have a-- she had a cousin in [INAUDIBLE].
So I see my brother and this.
And she contacted her cousin.
In London.
I was three years alone.
Again, [? sad ?] story.
I go-- when my wife died, I asked my children what we do-- what I do.
Bury or cremation?
Said, no.
We like mother to be buried.
It was difficult.
I was in [INAUDIBLE] house to get the permission to bury my wife in [?
Bushey. ?] [?
Said, ?] why will bury your wife in a Jewish cemetery?
Tell ?] [? you ?] what I can answer you.
She is a Jewish, and the children like bury her in a Jewish cemetery.
Though I wasn't a member of a synagogue.
So I must pay a lot of money backwards to be a member of a synagogue.
And I must bring a witness that my wife is a Jewish.
So my sister, my niece, her husband is the-- was the son from Rabbi Schonfield.
So I phoned my wife, he died in between.
My sister, she can give me the phone number from Rabbi Schonfield.
So I give them [? and ?] [?
I ?] phone him on the phone and ask about my wife.
And Rabbi Schonfield tell Mrs.
Tausinger, she was a better Jewish as all of you in the [INAUDIBLE] House.
So I buried my wife this.
So the first three years I go week by week every Sunday to the cemetery, to [?
Bushey. ?] I go to the grave and sit there.
I don't know.
After a few months, when I was there, stopped me the [?
Rabbi ?] from the [?
Bushey ?] [? cemetery ?] and told me, look.
I see you are here every week.
[INAUDIBLE] I don't like to see you here more.
You come here once in a year.
No not every Sunday.
You go between the life people.
It is your place.
Not in the cemetery.
So I stopped.
So after years, somebody introduce us.
And now I am-- how you say-- 20 years married.
What I told you-- the joke that I married-- I told you in the zoo.
A Jewish father with a son goes to the zoo where there was a lot of camels.
You know camel is English?
Yes?
Yes.
So the son ask the father, Daddy, camels marry too?
And the father said, only the camels marry.
So it is with me.
So I am now exactly 20 years in the second marriage.
In the first marriage I was 33 years.
So things got no troubles with the children, no troubles with the grandchildren.
I live my life, and the children live their own life.
No problems.
I visit my daughter here week by week or once in a fortnight.
And the Manchester people, comes two, three times in a year to London.
On the beginning where I was younger, I got to Manchester.
Now it is a little bit difficult.
Now I give up the driving.
I don't drive more the car.
After 65 years, stop driving.
Now I am, to be honest, is cheaper to take a minicab than to take the car.
The insurance, the repairs.
Always something.
Did you ever give up hope, Albert?
Sorry?
What you mean?
What hope?
No.
I don't give up the hope.
I don't think about it.
I am questioning this, what you are doing.
It must be only a few people around my age, and I will hope that the next generation, my grandchildren, they will be interested about the Holocaust and don't forget it.
Why ?] the children now are too busy with computers, with other things.
And I don't think they are much thinking about the Judaism.
So when my grandchildren, they go to the Sunday School-- from the one daughter only.
The other daughters, the-- the son and this, they are not so interested about [? their ?] religion.
So it is what I am thinking you do a very big job.
It's a very, very unbelievable.
I take my hat down.
A very big job.
The question is what I said-- the grandchildren and the next generation will be interested about the Holocaust.
It is the big question.
Well, thank you very much, Albert, for telling your story.
You've been a fascinating man to listen to.
Thank you for sharing it with us.
I hope it was all right.
It was very good.
Thank you.
With my difficult English.
Not at all.
Not at all.
